summary: Gruber corrects his previous theory about slow iOS 8 adoption, acknowledging that the primary cause isn't distrust of Apple's software quality but rather the 5 GB free space requirement for over-the-air updates. He received unprecedented reader feedback confirming that many users want to upgrade but can't because their 16 GB (or 8 GB) devices lack sufficient free space. He argues this is a serious, potentially permanent problem for iOS 8 adoption rates. Gruber connects this to his longstanding criticism of Apple's decision to keep 16 GB as the entry-level storage tier, calling it 'downright punitive' and suggesting that whatever profit Apple gains from this pricing strategy isn't worth the goodwill they're losing.
key_points: The 5 GB free space requirement for OTA updates is the primary barrier to iOS 8 adoption, not distrust of Apple's software quality
Casual users who rely on OTA updates don't know they can update via iTunes or free up space by offloading photos and videos to a computer
16 GB devices only have about 12 GB usable space after iOS itself, making the 5 GB update requirement nearly impossible for many users
Apple's decision to keep 16 GB as the base storage tier is a short-term profit play that damages long-term goodwill
These devices won't magically gain more storage, so iOS 8 adoption could lag permanently unless Apple reduces the update's space requirements
money_quotes: The simple answer was staring me right in the face. It's all about the over-the-air update requiring 5 GB of free storage space, and many people not having that much free space, and not knowing how or simply not wanting to deal with it.
I don't think I have ever received so much reader feedback on a post in the history of Daring Fireball.
iPhone makes it so easy for casual users to take gigabytes of photos and videos but nearly impossible for those users to know what to do with them.
A lot of these devices might never get updated to iOS 8, but would if the OTA software worked.
The entry-level iPhones 6 are 50 times faster than that original iPhone, but have only twice the storage capacity. That's just wrong.

If this decision was made simply in the interest of profit margins, and/or to nudge would-be entry-level-model buyers to the more expensive 64 GB mid-range models, whatever money Apple is making from this is not worth it, in the long run, compared to the collective goodwill they're losing and the frustration they're creating.
It's inexcusable that they're still selling new devices with only 8 GB of storage.
key_insight: Apple's insistence on selling 16 GB base-model iPhones created a self-inflicted wound where millions of users couldn't update to iOS 8 over the air, turning a pricing decision into an adoption crisis.
